title: Managing Participants and Reports
description: Update and delete participant accounts, understand system and business process implications of deletion, and generate audit and license usage reports.

topics.title: Managing Existing Participants

content.text: Participant administration is an ongoing responsibility. As users join and leave the organization, their accounts must be updated or removed. Understanding the correct procedures for modifying and deleting user accounts ensures that Windchill data integrity is maintained and that no work is lost during transitions.
Updating User Accounts

To update a user's account information, connect as the organization administrator and open the Participant Administration table. Search for the participant you need to modify, then select Edit from the context menu. You can change attributes such as the user's name, email address, and other profile fields. Changes take effect immediately and are reflected across all contexts where the user participates.
Preparing to Delete a User

content.prompt: Arrange the steps for preparing and completing a user deletion in the correct order.
items: Complete or reassign all user assignments
Check in or undo checkout of all work items
Delete the user's Personal Cabinet if necessary

content.text: You must clear all pending assignments and check in all work before deleting a user. Windchill will not allow deletion of an account that has outstanding assignments or checked-out items.

keyTakeaways: User accounts are updated through the Participant Administration table using the Edit context menu action.
All assignments must be completed or reassigned before a user can be deleted.
Checked-out items must be checked in or undone before deletion proceeds.
The user's Personal Cabinet should be deleted after the account is removed.

topics.title: Deleting Users and System Implications

content.text: Deleting a user account has wide-reaching effects across the Windchill system. The method of deletion determines what is cleaned up automatically and what requires manual intervention. Understanding these implications helps administrators avoid orphaned data and disrupted business processes.
Deletion Options and Effects

rows: Delete from Windchill and Directory Server
User removed
User object and all references removed
Delete from Windchill only
User entry remains
User object removed, Windchill maintenance performed
Delete from Directory Server only (non-Windchill tool)
User removed
User becomes disconnected participant - must be cleaned up manually

content.text: If a user is deleted from the directory server using an external LDAP tool rather than through Windchill, the user becomes a disconnected participant. This orphaned record must be found and cleaned up manually using the Participant Administration tool.
Impact on Running Processes

cards.back: User is automatically removed from role mappings in lifecycle templates, workflow templates, context teams, team templates, and team instances.
Future tasks are reassigned to the responsible role. If the deleted user held the responsible role, tasks go to the process initiator.
Deleting the Personal Cabinet removes all server-side workspaces and any work uploaded but not checked in. Items must be checked in first.

content.prompt: Match each consequence to the deletion method that causes it.

pairs.left: User can no longer sign in
User still appears in iteration history

keyTakeaways: Deleting from both Windchill and the directory server provides the cleanest removal of a user account.
Deleting a user from the directory server with a non-Windchill tool creates a disconnected participant that must be cleaned up manually.
Workflow tasks assigned to a deleted user are reassigned to the responsible role or the process initiator.
A deleted user still appears in iteration history but without an active email link.

topics.title: Deleting Groups and Organizations

content.text: Groups and organizations are foundational structures in Windchill's participant model. Deleting a group has cascading effects on access control, notifications, and role mappings. Organization contexts, however, have permanent implications that require careful upfront planning.
Impact of Deleting a Group

rows: Group membership
All users are disassociated from the group (users are not deleted)
Search results
Group no longer appears in most searches
Access control
All rules referencing the group are removed from every domain
Notifications
Group is removed from all notification lists
Role mappings
Group is removed from lifecycle, workflow, context team, and team template role mappings

content.text: Organization contexts cannot be deleted in Windchill. An organization participant cannot be deleted if it is associated with an organization context. Plan your organization structure carefully before implementation.
Best Practices

cards.back: Use a single organization context for medium-sized implementations. Multiple orgs are only needed for distinct, separate divisions that don't share data.
Identify whether to use a pre-established corporate LDAP or create a new one. Windchill needs the bundled directory server even when using corporate LDAP.
Always specify a valid email address - without it, notifications fail and users may not appear in search tools. Don't change default domain settings.

content.prompt: Match each participant management scenario to the correct concept.

pairs.left: Users are disassociated but not deleted
Must be cleaned up after external LDAP deletion

keyTakeaways: Deleting a group removes all access control rules that reference it across every domain.
Organization contexts cannot be deleted - plan your organizational structure before implementation.
Group deletion disassociates users but does not delete them from the system.
Always specify valid email addresses for users to ensure notifications and search functionality work correctly.

topics.title: Reports, Auditing, and License Usage

content.text: Windchill provides a suite of reporting and auditing tools that help business administrators monitor system usage, track security events, and manage license compliance. Reports can be generated in multiple formats including PDF, XML, HTML, and CSV, and can be customized using the Query Builder or third-party tools.
Report Authoring Tools

rows: Query Builder
Define and save queries against Windchill data using SQL concepts
Report templates displayed in Report Templates table
Jasper Report Studio
Open-source Java reporting tool for rich, flexible reports
PDF, Excel, CSV, XML, HTML reports
Info*Engine Tasks
Create data sources for Windchill Business Reporting
Custom data sources for reports

content.text: Do not edit predefined Windchill reports. If you need to customize a report, create a new one based on the existing template. This preserves the default reports for future reference and upgrades.
Security Auditing and License Usage

cards.back: View audit log purge history and create jobs to purge old audit records.
View license usage reports, edit license counts, and track license count change history.
Report the number of unique users accessing Windchill by year, month, week, day, or 30-minute intervals.
Create and view security audit reports, save report queries, and filter by event type, context, and user.

content.prompt: Arrange the Query Builder configuration steps in the correct order.

keyTakeaways: Windchill reports can be output in PDF, XML, HTML, and CSV formats using Query Builder, Jasper Report Studio, or Info*Engine Tasks.
The Query Builder uses SQL concepts organized across Properties, Tables and Joins, Select or Constrain, and Criteria tabs.
Security Audit Reporting allows filtering by event type, context, and user to track system access and changes.
Usage Reporting provides unique user counts filterable by time period for license compliance monitoring.

topics.title: Exercise - Delete a User Account

content.text: Harold Lloyd has resigned from the organization, and his user account must be fully removed from the system. In this exercise you will delete his Windchill and directory server account, then clean up his Personal Cabinet to ensure no orphaned data remains.

Before deleting any user account, verify that all checked-out work has been checked in or undone. Deleting the Personal Cabinet permanently removes all server-side workspaces and any unchecked-in work.

content.title: Delete a User Account
content.objective: Completely remove Harold Lloyd's user account from both the Windchill database and directory server, then clean up the Personal Cabinet.

steps.detail: Launch Windchill and sign in with wcadmin credentials.
Browse to Organizations > PTC Publications > Utilities and click Participant Administration.
Click Add participants to table, search for hlloyd, and add Lloyd Harold to the table.
Right-click the user and select Delete from Windchill and Windchill Directory Server. Confirm the action.
steps.hint: Verify the user no longer appears in the Participant Administration table.

steps.detail: Browse to Site > Utilities and click Personal Cabinets Administration in the System Administration section.
Click the Checked Out Work icon for hlloyd to verify no objects are checked out.
steps.hint: If objects are checked out, they must be checked in or undone before the cabinet can be deleted.

steps.detail: Select the checkbox for hlloyd and click Delete. Confirm the action.

keyTakeaways: User accounts should be deleted from both Windchill and the directory server for a complete removal.
The Personal Cabinet must be deleted separately after the user account is removed.
Always verify that no objects are checked out before deleting a Personal Cabinet to avoid data loss.

topics.title: Exercise - Execute Audit and License Reports

content.text: Business administrators regularly generate security audit and license usage reports for compliance reviews and management reporting. In this exercise you will create a security audit report filtered to login events and then generate a license usage report.
Sign in as wcadmin (site administrator) to access the auditing and license management tools on the Site Utilities page.

content.title: Execute Audit and License Reports
content.objective: Create a security audit report for login events and generate a license usage report for management review.

steps.detail: From the Site Utilities page, select Security Audit Reporting in the Auditing Administration section.
Set Duration to Current Year and set Only events of type to Login.
steps.hint: You can also filter by context, objects, and user for more targeted reports.

steps.detail: Click Preview to generate an HTML report showing sign-in actions from today's exercises.
steps.hint: Use Generate for full CSV or XML reports.

steps.detail: Click Site in the breadcrumb links and select License Management.
Select the License Information tab to view licenses with their types, quantities, and expiration dates.
steps.hint: Click the View information icon on any license to see profile details and members.

steps.detail: Select the License Notifications tab to verify any pending notifications.
Select the Report tab and generate the license report for the last three months. Open and review the downloaded report.
steps.hint: Note the active license groups, user counts, and the Deleted Users number.

keyTakeaways: Security Audit Reporting can filter events by type, duration, context, and user for targeted audit reports.
License Usage Reporting shows license types, quantities, expiration dates, and historical usage data.
The Preview option generates a quick HTML report while Generate produces full CSV or XML output.
